docs.title: HKPUG PyPI 30-Day CTF
docs.text:
Learn PyPI by hacking a safe toy package ecosystem.

This site is the participant-facing tutorial and progress hub. The GitHub repo contains the raw challenge files; this MkDocs site explains how to play, how each lab works, and how progress is tracked.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
1. Read How To Play.
  2. Open the Tutorial Index.
  3. Start with Flag 01.
  4. Check the Scoreboard.

docs.title: Learning Promise
docs.text:
Every flag is a hacking lab:

  • point pip at a challenge index
  • inspect /simple/ pages
  • make pip choose a controlled package
  • inspect wheels and source distributions
  • trigger harmless local flag capture
  • explain the defensive fix

No real PyPI uploads, no real package names, no real credentials.

docs.title: MVP Trail
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  F01["01 Install"] --> F02["02 Inspect Index"] --> F03["03 Normalize"]
  F03 --> F08["08 Confuse Resolver"] --> F11["11 Autopsy Wheel"]
  F11 --> F16["16 Legacy Build Trap"] --> F25["25 Patch Defense"]

docs.title: Participant Working Format
docs.text: This page explains how participants should work through the challenge.

docs.title: The Daily Loop
docs.text:
Each flag follows the same shape:

  1. read the scenario
  2. run the setup command
  3. inspect or modify the toy index/package
  4. make the controlled package capture the fake flag
  5. submit the flag and one short explanation
  6. optionally submit a defensive patch or writeup

The challenge is about learning PyPI by hacking a safe toy ecosystem.

docs.title: What "Hack" Means Here
docs.text:
Allowed:

  • point pip at the provided challenge index
  • build toy wheels or sdists inside this repo
  • edit challenge-owned index HTML files
  • run the victim app inside the challenge workspace
  • capture fake flags under artifacts/
  • inspect installed files in a temporary virtual environment

Not allowed:

  • uploading anything to real PyPI
  • targeting real package names
  • reading real credentials
  • contacting external callback servers
  • modifying files outside the challenge workspace

docs.title: Standard Lab Layout
docs.text:
Each lab should eventually follow this structure:

labs/flag-XX-name/
  README.md
  scenario/
  packages-src/
  indexes/
  victim/
  expected/

Some early tutorial labs may be lighter than this.

docs.title: Standard Flag Capture
docs.text:
The official fake flag is provided through a local environment variable:

export HKPUG_FAKE_FLAG="HKPUG{practice.flag-XX}"

The package or victim app should write proof to:

artifacts/flag-XX.txt

Example proof:

{
  "flag_id": "flag-08",
  "package": "hkpug-ctf-internal-utils",
  "version": "9.9.9",
  "source_index": "public-sim",
  "captured_flag": "HKPUG{practice.flag-08}"
}

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
For the MVP, participants submit:

  • flag value
  • one or two sentence explanation
  • optional patch or writeup

For hosted HKPUG mode, answers are encrypted before a pull request.

For community mode, participants can self-check locally and submit a public writeup after the official window closes.

docs.title: E-Cert Progress
docs.text:
Suggested thresholds:

  • Participation: 3 valid flags
  • Explorer: 10 valid flags across at least 2 tracks
  • Completion: 21 valid flags across at least 5 tracks
  • Finisher: capstone plus defense checklist

docs.title: Scoreboard
docs.text:
This scoreboard is intentionally friendly. It should show progress and learning status before pure ranking.

The first version reads static data from:

assets/leaderboard.json

Later, the trusted scorer can update the same JSON file on the Pages branch.

Filter Loading scoreboard...

docs.title: Certificate Tiers
docs.text: Tier Suggested Requirement Participation 3 valid flags Explorer 10 valid flags across at least 2 tracks Completion 21 valid flags across at least 5 tracks Finisher capstone plus defense checklist

docs.title: Lab Index
docs.text:
The first playable version should focus on the MVP trail.

Flag Lab Status 01 Custom Index Install stub 02 Manual Simple API stub 03 Name Normalization stub 08 Extra Index Confusion stub 11 Wheel Autopsy stub 16 Legacy Sdist Trap stub 25 Safer Index Patch stub

Each lab should include:

  • scenario
  • objective
  • setup commands
  • expected artifact or output
  • hints
  • defense notes

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You are given a package that is not on real PyPI. Your first job is to install it from the challenge index.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Make pip install hkpug-ctf-hello from the trusted challenge index and print the practice flag.

docs.title: Working Notes
docs.text:
This lab should eventually point to:

labs/flag-01-custom-index-install/

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• captured flag
  • install command used
  • one sentence explaining why real PyPI was not involved

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: Before using pip, inspect the index by hand. The project page is just HTML that links to package files.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Find the wheel URL and hash for hkpug-ctf-hello by reading the simple index pages.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• project page path
  • wheel filename
  • hash fragment or computed hash
  • practice flag

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The same package can be requested with different punctuation. Pip normalizes the name before looking up the project page.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Show that package name variants resolve to the same normalized project name.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• normalized name
  • package variants tested
  • practice flag

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The victim app trusts a private index but also uses a simulated public index. The same package name exists in both places.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Make pip choose the higher-version package from public-sim and capture the fake flag locally.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• captured flag
  • selected package version
  • which index won
  • defensive fix

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: A wheel was installed. Before trusting it, inspect it.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Unzip the wheel and find the hidden metadata flag.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• metadata flag
  • distribution name
  • version
  • one suspicious or important file found

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The victim is forced to install from a source distribution. A legacy setup.py path writes a local marker during the controlled build.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Trigger the legacy source-build path and capture the fake flag under artifacts/.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• captured flag
  • whether pip used wheel or sdist
  • which build phase created the marker
  • one modern packaging mitigation

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You exploited an unsafe install configuration. Now fix it.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Patch the victim app so it no longer resolves internal packages from the simulated public index.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• patched command or config
  • explanation of why the old config was unsafe
  • practice flag from the fixed self-check

docs.title: MkDocs And Scoreboard Plan
docs.text:
Use the same repository:

  • main stores source docs, labs, and challenge assets
  • gh-pages stores the generated MkDocs site
  • GitHub Pages serves the tutorial site

This keeps the public working repo and participant tutorial site in sync.

docs.title: Scoreboard Model
docs.text:
The MkDocs page at scoreboard.md reads:

docs/assets/leaderboard.json

During development, this file contains demo data.

In hosted mode, the trusted scorer should update the generated Pages branch with a real assets/leaderboard.json or commit the source JSON and redeploy.

Recommended fields:

{
  "team_id": "hk-01",
  "display_name": "Team Name",
  "region": "Hong Kong",
  "flags": 7,
  "tracks": 3,
  "tier": "Explorer",
  "latest_flag": "flag-25",
  "score": 70,
  "certificate_eligible": true
}

docs.title: GitHub Pages Deployment
docs.text:
The workflow should:

  1. install MkDocs dependencies
  2. build the site
  3. publish the generated site to the gh-pages branch
  4. serve GitHub Pages from that branch

When the repo is ready to go public, enable Pages from the gh-pages branch in repository settings.

docs.title: Tutorial Pages
docs.text:
The 20260530 workshop showed that participants need more guidance on the general working format. These tutorials should be short, practical, and linked from each lab.

Recommended reading order:

  1. 00-how-to-play.md
  2. 01-pip-index-basics.md
  3. 02-package-build-artifacts.md
  4. 03-legacy-vs-modern-packaging.md
  5. 04-submission-and-certificates.md

Each tutorial should answer three questions:

  • What am I looking at?
  • What command do I run?
  • What should I observe?

docs.title: How To Play
docs.text:
This challenge is a safe PyPI hacking trail.

You will:

  • create or inspect toy package indexes
  • make pip resolve packages from controlled indexes
  • inspect wheels and source distributions
  • trigger harmless local flag capture
  • explain the fix

You will not:

  • upload packages to real PyPI
  • attack real packages
  • use real credentials
  • exfiltrate anything

docs.title: Basic Workflow
docs.text:
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -r requirements.txt

Then open a lab:

cd labs/flag-01-custom-index-install

Read the lab README.md, run the setup command, and capture the fake flag.

docs.title: Where Flags Go
docs.text:
Most labs write proof under:

artifacts/

If a lab prints a flag instead, copy the printed value into your answer bundle.

docs.title: When You Are Stuck
docs.text:
Look for:

  • the pip install command
  • the --index-url and --extra-index-url values
  • the project page under /simple/<normalized-name>/
  • the selected version
  • whether pip chose a wheel or sdist

docs.title: Pip Index Basics
docs.text:
When you run pip install SomePackage, pip does not magically know where the package file is. It asks a package index.

The default index is PyPI, but in this challenge we point pip at local or hosted toy indexes.

docs.title: Important Options
docs.text:
Use one index:

python -m pip install --index-url file://$PWD/indexes/trusted/simple hkpug-ctf-hello

Add another index:

python -m pip install \
  --index-url file://$PWD/indexes/trusted/simple \
  --extra-index-url file://$PWD/indexes/public-sim/simple \
  hkpug-ctf-hello

The second form is where dependency confusion lessons start.

docs.title: Simple Index Shape
docs.text:
A minimal index looks like:

indexes/trusted/simple/
  index.html
  hkpug-ctf-hello/
    index.html
packages/
  hkpug_ctf_hello-1.0.0-py3-none-any.whl

The project page links to distribution files.

docs.title: Name Normalization
docs.text:
Package names are normalized for index lookup. Runs of ., _, and - become one -, and letters are lowercased.

Examples:

  • HKPUG_CTF.Hello
  • hkpug-ctf-hello
  • hkpug.ctf_hello

All normalize to:

hkpug-ctf-hello

docs.title: Package Build Artifacts
docs.text: Python packages commonly arrive as wheels or source distributions.

docs.title: Wheel
docs.text:
A wheel is a built artifact. It is a zip file with metadata.

Useful files:

  • METADATA
  • WHEEL
  • RECORD
  • package files

Inspect one:

python -m zipfile --list dist/example.whl
python -m zipfile --extract dist/example.whl /tmp/example-wheel

docs.title: Source Distribution
docs.text:
An sdist contains source that may need to be built before install.

That build step is the interesting part for several labs. Legacy setup.py source builds can run project build logic in ways participants should learn to recognize.

docs.title: Lab Question
docs.text:
For any artifact, ask:

  • Where did pip download it from?
  • Is it a wheel or sdist?
  • What version did pip select?
  • What code ran during build, install, import, or command execution?

docs.title: Legacy Setup.py Versus Modern Packaging
docs.text: This challenge includes old and modern packaging flows.

docs.title: Legacy Pattern
docs.text:
Older packages often use setup.py directly. This can mix:

  • metadata
  • dependency logic
  • build logic
  • arbitrary Python execution

That is why several labs ask you to compare wheel installs, sdist installs, and direct setup.py behavior.

docs.title: Modern Pattern
docs.text:
Modern packages usually declare build requirements in pyproject.toml:

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

Modern build frontends can inspect build requirements before building and can build in isolated environments.

docs.title: Security Lesson
docs.text:
Modern packaging is not magic. A malicious package can still run when imported or executed.

But modern packaging helps because:

  • build requirements are explicit
  • build isolation narrows accidental environment access
  • wheels avoid project build logic at install time
  • hashes and lockfiles help detect artifact substitution
  • trusted publishing avoids long-lived release tokens

docs.title: Submission And Certificates
docs.text: The hosted HKPUG mode should use encrypted answer submission. The community mode can use local self-checks.

docs.title: Official Submission
docs.text:
Participants submit:

  • encrypted answers
  • signed manifest
  • optional encrypted writeup

The trusted scorer decrypts answers, checks flags, updates the progress board, and records certificate eligibility.

docs.title: Community Submission
docs.text:
Community participants can:

  • solve locally
  • keep notes
  • publish writeups after the official window
  • use public practice flags

docs.title: Certificate Tiers
docs.text:
Suggested tiers:

  • Participation: 3 valid flags
  • Explorer: 10 valid flags
  • Completion: 21 valid flags
  • Finisher: capstone plus defense checklist

Registration should collect a certificate display name separately from GitHub username.
